title: Retirement Calculator for Missouri Residents: 2026 Guide
metaDescription: Free retirement calculator for Missouri residents. Account for 4.8% top tax rate, Social Security taxation, COL index 87, and MOSERS / PSRS in your Missouri retirement plan.

content.introduction:
Retirement planning in Missouri isn't just about how much you save — it's about how much you keep. The Show-Me State has a progressive income tax with rates up to 4.8%, which directly impacts your retirement withdrawals. With a cost of living index of 87 (100 = national average), your retirement dollar goes further in the Show-Me State.

A retirement calculator for missouri residents needs to factor in these state-specific variables — not just generic 7% returns and 4% withdrawal rates. This guide covers exactly how Missouri's tax structure, pension systems, Social Security treatment, and cost of living change your retirement math, with worked examples using real Missouri numbers.

sections.heading: Missouri's Retirement Tax Landscape
sections.content:
Missouri taxes retirement income at rates up to 4.8%, and also taxes Social Security benefits. Missouri partially taxes Social Security: exempt if AGI is under $85,000 (single) or $100,000 (married). Above those thresholds, partial taxation applies.

Key tax factors for Missouri retirees:

  • Income Tax: 2% to 4.8% across 10 brackets; top rate applies above $8,968
  • Social Security: Missouri partially taxes Social Security: exempt if AGI is under $85,000 (single) or $100,000 (married). Above those thresholds, partial taxation applies.
  • Estate/Inheritance Tax: No state estate or inheritance tax.
  • Senior Property Tax Relief: Property Tax Credit for 65+ renters/homeowners with income under $30,000 — credit of up to $1,100.

On a $40,000 annual withdrawal, Missouri retirees could owe up to $1,920 in state income tax — a meaningful drag on retirement income. Use our retirement calculator to see how this affects your specific numbers.

sections.heading: How Much Do You Need to Retire in Missouri?
sections.content:
The answer depends on your lifestyle, but here's the math using Missouri-specific cost data:

  • Median household income: $61,043
  • Cost of living index: 87 (below national average)
  • Target retirement income: 80% of pre-retirement income = $42,486/year
  • Portfolio needed (4% rule): $1,062,150

The 4% rule means withdrawing 4% of your portfolio annually, adjusted for inflation. For Missouri residents earning the median income, you'd need roughly $1,062,150 invested at retirement to maintain your current lifestyle — plus an additional buffer for state income taxes of up to 4.8%.

Social Security replaces roughly 40% of pre-retirement income for average earners, potentially reducing your needed portfolio to around $637,290.

sections.heading: MOSERS / PSRS: State Pension Benefits
sections.content:
Missouri's public pension system — MOSERS / PSRS — plays a significant role for public employees. Missouri State Employees Retirement System and Public School Retirement System. Defined benefit plans.

If you're a Missouri public employee, your retirement calculator inputs change significantly:

  • Pension income: Typically replaces 50-70% of final average salary for career employees
  • Social Security interaction: Some Missouri public employees don't pay into Social Security — your pension is the primary replacement
  • Supplemental savings: Even with a pension, most financial planners recommend building a 457(b) or supplemental retirement account for flexibility

For private-sector workers, your retirement income comes entirely from personal savings (401(k), IRA, taxable accounts) plus Social Security. The retirement calculator helps you model exactly how much to save each month.

sections.heading: Retirement Savings Timeline for Missouri Workers
sections.content:
Using Missouri's median household income of $61,043 and a 15% savings rate:

Start AgeMonthly SavingsPortfolio at 65Monthly Income (4% rule)
25$763$2,002,733$6,676
30$763$1,374,205$4,581
35$763$930,838$3,103
40$763$618,085$2,060
45$763$397,467$1,325

Starting at 25 vs 35 nearly doubles your retirement portfolio — that's the power of compound interest over an extra decade. Every year of delay costs approximately $71,572 in final portfolio value.

Explore different timelines with our compound interest calculator to see exactly how compounding works for your situation.

sections.heading: Missouri Cost of Living Impact on Retirement
sections.content:
Missouri's cost of living index of 87 means retirees benefit from costs that are 13% below the national average. Your retirement savings stretch further in the Show-Me State.

What this means for your retirement number:

  • National benchmark: $1 million portfolio → $40,000/year (4% rule)
  • Adjusted for Missouri: $1 million portfolio → effectively $45,977/year in purchasing power
  • Missouri-adjusted target: To match $40,000 national purchasing power, you need $870,000

Missouri's affordability is a real advantage — your savings go further here than in many other states.

sections.heading: Tax-Advantaged Accounts: Maximize Your Missouri Savings
sections.content:
Regardless of where you live, maximize these accounts before taxable investing:

  • 401(k)/403(b): $24,000 limit in 2026 ($32,000 if 50+) — employer match is free money
  • Traditional IRA: $7,500 limit ($8,500 if 50+) — tax-deductible, reducing your Missouri state tax bill
  • Roth IRA: $7,500 limit — grows tax-free forever. Particularly valuable in Missouri: you skip the 4.8% state tax on all future withdrawals.
  • HSA: $4,350 individual / $8,750 family — triple tax advantage for healthcare costs in retirement

529 Plans: Up to $8,000 ($16,000 married) deductible for MOST 529 contributions.

For Missouri residents, the Roth IRA is especially compelling: paying 4.8% state tax now to avoid it on decades of growth and withdrawals often makes mathematical sense.

sections.heading: Common Retirement Planning Mistakes in Missouri
sections.content:
• Ignoring state tax changes: Missouri's tax rates can change. Plan for current rates but build flexibility into your strategy.
  • Underestimating healthcare costs: The average 65-year-old couple needs $315,000+ for healthcare in retirement, and this figure applies nationally regardless of COL
  • Forgetting inflation: At 3% inflation, $40,000/year buying power shrinks to $24,000 in 15 years. Your calculator should model inflation-adjusted withdrawals.
  • Over-relying on Social Security: The average Social Security benefit is ~$1,900/month. For Missouri residents, that covers only 54% of estimated expenses.
  • Not accounting for Missouri-specific costs: Heating costs and severe weather insurance can surprise Missouri retirees.

content.practicalExample:
Retirement Scenario: Missouri Worker, Age 35

Profile: A 35-year-old Missouri resident earning $61,043/year, saving 15% for retirement.

Inputs:

  • Monthly contribution: $763
  • Years to retirement: 30
  • Expected return: 7% (historical stock market average)
  • Current savings: $50,000

Results at Age 65:

  • Portfolio value: $930,838
  • Annual withdrawal (4% rule): $37,234
  • Monthly retirement income: $3,103
  • Est. state tax on withdrawals: -$74/month (effective rate ~2%)
  • Plus Social Security (~$1,900/month at full retirement age)

Bottom line: Combined retirement income of ~$5,003/month before state taxes — sufficient to cover estimated expenses.

Model your own scenario with our retirement calculator.
content.conclusion:
Retirement planning for Missouri residents requires more than plugging numbers into a generic calculator. The Show-Me State's 4.8% top income tax rate, Social Security taxation, cost of living index of 87, and pension system (MOSERS / PSRS) all materially change how much you need and how long your savings will last.

Start with our retirement calculator to model your specific situation — input your actual income, savings rate, and expected retirement age to see where you stand. Then use the compound interest calculator to visualize how starting today versus next year impacts your final number.

faqs.question: Does Missouri tax Social Security benefits?
faqs.answer: Missouri partially taxes Social Security: exempt if AGI is under $85,000 (single) or $100,000 (married). Above those thresholds, partial taxation applies. This means Social Security recipients in Missouri should factor state taxes into their retirement income projections.

faqs.question: What is a good retirement savings target for Missouri?
faqs.answer: Based on Missouri's cost of living index of 87 and median income of $61,043, a household targeting 80% income replacement needs approximately $1,062,150 in retirement savings (using the 4% rule). Social Security reduces this by roughly 40%, bringing the savings target to about $637,290. Adjust based on your actual expenses, healthcare needs, and desired lifestyle.

faqs.question: Is Missouri a good state to retire in?
faqs.answer: Missouri has a 4.8% top tax rate on retirement income, which is moderate and offset by other benefits. The cost of living index of 87 means your money goes further than the national average. Consider: Property Tax Credit for 65+ renters/homeowners with income under $30,000 — credit of up to $1,100.

faqs.question: How does MOSERS / PSRS work?
faqs.answer: Missouri State Employees Retirement System and Public School Retirement System. Defined benefit plans. Missouri's pension system covers public employees including teachers, state workers, and often local government employees. Benefits are typically calculated as a formula: years of service multiplied by a percentage (often 1.5-2.5%) multiplied by final average salary. Contact MOSERS / PSRS directly for your specific benefit estimate, and use our retirement calculator to model how pension income supplements your personal savings.
